---
title: "Litter Effects in BMABMDR"
---

## Quantal Data

The key distinction is the **data format**:

- **Standard quantal** (`data_quantal`): one row per dose group, columns = `dose`, `response` (total affected), `size` (total N)
- **Quantal with litter** (`data_quantal_litter`): one row per **litter**, columns = `dose`, `y` (affected in that litter), `n` (litter size)

```{r, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
library(BMABMDR)

# --- Standard quantal (no litter): one row per dose group ---
data("data_quantal")
data_quantal
#>   dose response size
#> 1    0        0   20
#> 2    5        4   20
#> 3   15        6   20
#> 4   50        5   20
#> 5  100       12   20

data_Q <- PREP_DATA_QA(data = data_quantal, sumstats = TRUE, q = 0.1)
fit_q  <- full.laplaceQ_MA(data.Q = data_Q, prior.weights = rep(1, 8))
fit_q$MA

# --- Quantal with litter: one row per litter ---
data("data_quantal_litter")
head(data_quantal_litter)
#>   dose y  n
#> 1    0 1 12
#> 2    0 1 12
#> 3    0 0 12
#> ...
# Each row = one litter: y = affected pups, n = litter size

data_QL <- PREP_DATA_QA(data = data_quantal_litter,
                         sumstats = TRUE,   # each row is a litter summary
                         q = 0.1,
                         cluster = TRUE)    # activates Beta-Binomial
fit_ql  <- full.laplaceQ_MA(data.Q = data_QL, prior.weights = rep(1, 8))
fit_ql$MA

# --- Converting individual binary data to litter-level format ---
# Suppose you have raw individual binary outcomes:
set.seed(123)
indiv_quantal <- data.frame(
  dose   = rep(c(0, 5, 15, 50, 100), each = 200),
  litter = rep(rep(1:10, each = 20), 5),  # 10 litters per dose, 20 pups each
  y      = c(rbinom(200, 1, 0.05),        # dose 0:   5% background
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.10),         # dose 5:  10%
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.20),         # dose 15: 20%
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.35),         # dose 50: 35%
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.60))         # dose 100: 60%
)
head(indiv_quantal)
#>   dose litter y
#> 1    0      1 0
#> 2    0      1 0
#> ...

# Aggregate to litter-level (one row per litter)
library(dplyr)
litter_quantal <- indiv_quantal |>
  group_by(dose, litter) |>
  summarise(
    y = sum(y),     # number affected in this litter
    n = n(),        # litter size
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(dose, y, n) |>  # drop litter ID — PREP_DATA_QA doesn't use it
  as.data.frame()

litter_quantal
#>    dose  y  n
#> 1     0  1 20
#> 2     0  0 20
#> 3     0  2 20
#> 4     5  3 20
#> ...  (10 litters × 5 doses = 50 rows)

# Now fit with litter effect
data_QL2 <- PREP_DATA_QA(data = litter_quantal,
                          sumstats = TRUE,
                          q = 0.1,
                          cluster = TRUE)
fit_ql2  <- full.laplaceQ_MA(data.Q = data_QL2, prior.weights = rep(1, 8))
fit_ql2$MA
```

**The conversion pipeline:** individual binary → `group_by(dose, litter)` → `summarise(y = sum(y), n = n())` → pass to `PREP_DATA_QA(..., cluster = TRUE)`.

The `cluster = TRUE` flag switches the likelihood from Binomial to Beta-Binomial, adding the ICC parameter $\rho$ that accounts for within-litter correlation.

## Continuous Data

For continuous endpoints measured on individual animals within litters (or cages),
BMABMDR uses a **multivariate Normal likelihood with compound-symmetry** correlation
structure.  An extra parameter $\rho$ (intra-litter correlation) captures the
within-cluster dependence.

Key differences from the standard (non-clustered) continuous workflow:

| Aspect | Standard | Clustered |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Data level | Summary (Mean/SD/N per dose) | **Individual** (one row per animal) |
| Columns required | dose, response, size | **dose, response, litter** |
| Prep function | `PREP_DATA_N()` / `PREP_DATA_LN()` | `PREP_DATA_N_C()` / `PREP_DATA_LN_C()` |
| Fit function | `full.laplace_MA()` | `full.laplace_MAc()` |
| Models | 16 (8 shapes × {Normal, Lognormal}) | **16** (same shapes, same error families) |
| Extra parameter | — | $\rho$ (ICC, PERT prior on $[0,1]$) |

### Real-data example: Egg-Shell Thickness

The package ships `EggShellThickness.csv`, a study where hens were exposed to
different concentrations and eggshell thickness was measured per cage.

```{r, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
library(BMABMDR)

# EggShellThickness.csv lives in data/ in the source tree (not inst/extdata),
# so it is not installed with the package. We locate it relative to the
# vignette source directory.
eggshell_path <- here::here("data", "EggShellThickness.csv")
if (!file.exists(eggshell_path)) {
  # Fallback when rendering from vignettes/

  eggshell_path <- file.path("..", "data", "EggShellThickness.csv")
}
eggshell <- read.csv(eggshell_path)

head(eggshell)
#>   Exposure Cage Eggshell.Thickness
#> 1        0    1              0.191
#> 2        0    2              0.200

# Columns must be in order: dose, response, litter (cluster)
egg_input <- data.frame(
  dose     = eggshell$Exposure,
  response = eggshell[["Eggshell.Thickness"]],
  litter   = eggshell$Cage
)

# Prepare data for Normal and Lognormal clustered models
data_N  <- PREP_DATA_N_C(egg_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
data_LN <- PREP_DATA_LN_C(egg_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")

# Fit model-averaged BMD (Laplace)
fit_c <- full.laplace_MAc(data.N = data_N, data.LN = data_LN,
                           prior.weights = rep(1, 16))
fit_c$MA
```

### Foetal-weight example (`das5`)

The built-in `das5` dataset contains individual foetal weights nested within
litters.  This is the example used in `?PREP_DATA_N_C`:

```{r}
data(das5)
data_input <- data.frame(
  dose     = das5$data$dose,
  response = das5$data$foetalweight,
  litter   = das5$data$number
)
head(data_input)

data_N2  <- PREP_DATA_N_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
data_LN2 <- PREP_DATA_LN_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")

fit_c2 <- full.laplace_MAc(data.N = data_N2, data.LN = data_LN2,
                             prior.weights = rep(1, 16))
fit_c2$MA
```

### Simulated example: individual data with litter structure

If your data does not already have a cluster identifier you can simulate the
workflow end-to-end:

```{r}
set.seed(42)
n_litters  <- 5          # litters per dose group
litter_sz  <- 10         # pups per litter
doses      <- c(0, 10, 50, 100, 200)

sim_cont <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(doses), function(i) {
  d <- doses[i]
  do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_litters, function(j) {
    # Litter-level random intercept
    litter_effect <- rnorm(1, mean = 0, sd = 0.3)
    # True dose-response: background 10, decreasing
    mu <- 10 - 3 * (d / max(doses)) + litter_effect
    data.frame(
      dose     = d,
      response = rnorm(litter_sz, mean = mu, sd = 0.5),
      litter   = paste0("D", d, "_L", j)
    )
  }))
}))

head(sim_cont)

# Prepare & fit
data_N3  <- PREP_DATA_N_C(sim_cont, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
data_LN3 <- PREP_DATA_LN_C(sim_cont, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")

fit_c3 <- full.laplace_MAc(data.N = data_N3, data.LN = data_LN3,
                             prior.weights = rep(1, 16))
fit_c3$MA
```

**Key points:**

- Data must be **individual-level** (one row per animal), with column order
  `dose`, `response`, `litter`.
- `PREP_DATA_N_C()` / `PREP_DATA_LN_C()` replace the standard `PREP_DATA_N()` /
  `PREP_DATA_LN()`.
- `full.laplace_MAc()` replaces `full.laplace_MA()`.
- The Stan models internally estimate $\rho$, the intra-litter correlation, using
  a compound-symmetry covariance structure.
